3.4 Evaluate DIA-NN Results Let’s look at the data in this file. To do so, we’ll use head() to look at the first five rows of the data frame. head(diann_results) ## # A tibble: 6 × 71 ## Run.Index Run Channel Precursor.Id Modified.Sequence Stripped.Sequence ## <int> <chr> <chr> <chr> <chr> <chr> ## 1 0 Ahmed_WT3_… "" AAAAEIAVK2 AAAAEIAVK AAAAEIAVK ## 2 1 Ahmed_bisC… "" AAAAEIAVK2 AAAAEIAVK AAAAEIAVK ## 3 2 Ahmed_bisC… "" AAAAEIAVK2 AAAAEIAVK AAAAEIAVK ## 4 3 Ahmed_bisC… "" AAAAEIAVK2 AAAAEIAVK AAAAEIAVK ## 5 4 Ahmed_msrA… "" AAAAEIAVK2 AAAAEIAVK AAAAEIAVK ## 6 5 Ahmed_msrA… "" AAAAEIAVK2 AAAAEIAVK AAAAEIAVK ## # ℹ 65 more variables: Precursor.Charge <int>, Precursor.Lib.Index <int>, ## # Decoy <int>, Proteotypic <int>, Precursor.Mz <dbl>, Protein.Ids <chr>, ## # Protein.Group <chr>, Protein.Names <chr>, Genes <chr>, RT <dbl>, iRT <dbl>, ## # Predicted.RT <dbl>, Predicted.iRT <dbl>, IM <dbl>, iIM <dbl>, ## # Predicted.IM <dbl>, Predicted.iIM <dbl>, Precursor.Quantity <dbl>, ## # Precursor.Normalised <dbl>, Ms1.Area <dbl>, Ms1.Normalised <dbl>, ## # Ms1.Apex.Area <dbl>, Ms1.Apex.Mz.Delta <dbl>, Normalisation.Factor <dbl>, … 3.4.1 Explaining the DIA-NN Report Each row is a precursor ID (i.e., a charged peptide…remember you can have multiple precursors for a peptide) and each row is data specific to that precursor. A brief description of some of the important rows are below: Run: the run name the precursor was identified from Precursor.Id: the amino acid sequence followed by charge state of the precursor. Note that if the precursor is modified, the UniMod number will be here as well Modified.Sequence: the amino acid sequence of the precursor. Note that is the that if the precursor is modified, the UniMod number will be here as well Note: it is possible to have multiple Precursor.Ids for the same Modified.Sequence (e.g., the modified peptide was identified in both the +2 and +3 form) Stripped.Sequence: the amino acid sequence of the precursor. Note that if the precursor is modified, the UniMod number will NOT be here. Note: it is possible to have multiple Modified.Sequences for the same Stripped.Sequence (e.g., the peptide was identified in both the modified and un-modified form) Precursor.Charge: the charge state of the precursor Proteotypic: the precursor sequence matches to a single protein in the FASTA file. (1 = TRUE, 0 = FALSE) Precursor.mz: the m/z of the identified precursor Protein.Ids: The UniProt accession number(s) of all possible proteins the precursor matched to (i.e., every protein this precursor could have come from) Protein.Group: DIA-NN’s best attempt at resolving which minimal set of proteins can explain the identified precursor (i.e., what DIA-NN thinks the precursor most likely came from) Genes: The name of the gene that produces the “Protein.Group” RT: The retention time, in minutes, of the apex for the precursor IM: The ion mobility (if the instrument has it) of the precursor Precursor.Quantity: The un-normalized abundance of the precursor Precursor.Normalised: The normalized abundance of the precursor. Note that if normalization is disabled. the column ios simply the same value as “Precursor.Quantity” Quantity.Quality: The quality of the precursor quantification (higher value = higher confidence) RT.Start: The retention time, in minutes, of the first scan the precursor was detected RT.Stop: The retention time, in minutes, of the last scan the precursor was detected FWHM: The “Full-width, half-maximum” of the precursor, in minutes. This gives a measurement for how wide the precursor extracted ion chromatogram (XIC) PG.MaxLFQ: The abundance of the entire protein group that precursor matched to. This is calculated by using “MaxLFQ”, which tries to use all the possible precursor abundances across all the runs to find the most representative abundance that describes that protein group’s abundance for each sample. Note: this value will be the same for all precursors that match to the same protein group within each sample PG.MaxLFQ.Quality: The quality of the MaxLFQ quantification (high value = higher confidence) Q.Value: The confidence that the precursor identification within each run was a true identification (lower value = higher confidence) PEP: “Posterior Error Probability” (lower value = higher confidence) Global.Q.Value: The confidence that the precursor identification across all runs in the experiment was a true identification (lower value = higher confidence) Lib.Q.Value: The confidence that the precursor identification was matched to a precursor from the spectral library (lower value = higher confidence) Note: this only populate if MBR was enabled PG.Q.Value: The confidence that the protein group identification within each run was a true identification (lower value = higher confidence) Global.PG.Q.Value: The confidence that the protein group identification across all runs in the experiment was a true identification (lower value = higher confidence) Lib.PG.Q.Value: The confidence that the protein group identification was matched to a protein group from the spectral library (lower value = higher confidence) Note: this only populate if MBR was enabled Best.Fr.Mz: The m/z of the best fragment ion for this precursor. This isn’t always used, but will be necessary if you wish to use MSStats 3.4.2 Evaluating the Quality of the Runs Using some of the columns mentioned above, we are going to look at the quality of each of the precursors, and filter to only retain high quality IDs, and then re-check those same metrics. But first, we’re going to modify the diann_results data frame slightly: We will rename each Run so that we remove any common strings (“Ahmed_” and “_25-023”). This will make visualization slightly easier We will remove any unwanted columns so that we only have the ones mentioned above. The makes the data frame considerably smaller, making each subsequent step just a bit faster We will also be adding an extra column denoting the group of each sample. This will make it slightly easier to comprehend the graphs later on diann_results <- diann_results %>% select(c(Run, Precursor.Id:Precursor.Charge, Proteotypic:Protein.Group, Genes:RT, Precursor.Quantity, Precursor.Normalised, Quantity.Quality, RT.Stop:FWHM, PG.MaxLFQ, PG.MaxLFQ.Quality, Q.Value:Lib.Q.Value, PG.Q.Value, Global.PG.Q.Value, Lib.PG.Q.Value)) %>% mutate(Run = gsub('Ahmed_', '', Run), Run = gsub('_25-023', '', Run)) %>% # We are going to add "metadata" to our runs, which includes information such as the group that sample belongs to, the replicate, etc. We can make this beforehand, or simply make a new tibble that contains all the information, which we're doing here. So we will be making a new tibble where one column is all of the run names in our experiment ("Runs") and then another column that has the genotype of that samples ("group") left_join(y = tibble(Run = c( 'bisC1', 'bisC2', 'bisC3', 'msrA1', 'msrA2', 'msrA3', 'msrB1', 'msrB2', 'msrB3', 'msrC1', 'msrC2', 'msrC3', 'WT1', 'WT2', 'WT3'), group = c(rep('bisC', 3), rep('msrA', 3), rep('msrB', 3), rep('msrC', 3), rep('WT', 3))), by = 'Run') 3.4.2.1 Proteome Coverage Now let’s look to see how many precursors, modified peptides, and protein groups were identified in each experiment. Ideally, they should be pretty similar, or at least each biological group should be similar among each other. First, we will look at the number of precursors identified in each sample. While it would be nice to have all samples have a similar number, sometimes biology gets in the way. At the very least, we should expect each replicate from the same group to have a similar number of IDs. We’ll do this by looking at three different ID “levels”: precursors, peptides, and proteins. Precursor IDs diann_results %>% # We want only the columns "Run", "group", and "Precursor.Id". More specifically, only rows where all three of these columns are unique distinct(Run, group, Precursor.Id) %>% group_by(Run, group) %>% summarise(n_precursor_ids = n(), #counts the number of precursor IDs in each group (run and group) .groups = 'drop') %>% # We want to plot the number of unique precursors in each sample, grouping them by genotype ggplot(mapping = aes(x = Run, # x-axis will be the run y = n_precursor_ids, # y-axis will be the number of precursor IDs fill = group)) + # we want each genotype to be a different color geom_col() + # Draw a bar graph # Add labels to the plot labs(x = 'Sample', # x-axis title y ='Number of Precursor IDs', # y-axis title title = 'Number of Precursor IDs per Run') #Title of the plot Peptide IDs diann_results %>% distinct(Run, group, Modified.Sequence) %>% group_by(Run, group) %>% summarise(n_modified_peptides = n(), .groups = 'drop') %>% ggplot(mapping = aes(x = Run, y = n_modified_peptides, fill = group)) + geom_col() + labs(x = 'Sample', y ='Number of Modified Peptide IDs', title = 'Number of Modified Peptide IDs per Run') Protein IDs diann_results %>% distinct(Run, group, Stripped.Sequence) %>% group_by(Run, group) %>% summarise(n_peptides = n(), .groups = 'drop') %>% ggplot(mapping = aes(x = Run, y = n_peptides, fill = group)) + geom_col() + labs(x = 'Sample', y ='Number of Peptide IDs', title = 'Number of Unique Peptide IDs per Run') diann_results %>% distinct(Run, group, Protein.Group) %>% group_by(Run, group) %>% summarise(n_protein_groups = n(), .groups = 'drop') %>% ggplot(mapping = aes(x = Run, y = n_protein_groups, fill = group)) + geom_col() + labs(x = 'Sample', y ='Number of Protein Groups', title = 'Number of Protein Group IDs per Run') This series of plots show us a few things: The number of IDs decreases as we go up in “biological level” (i.e., precursor -> modified peptide -> stripped peptide -> protein). This inherently makes sense since multiple precursors can be produced from a single modified peptide, if there is a variable modification site, a peptide can exist as both modified and unmodified, and lastly multiple peptides can be produced from a single protein There seems to be one run, msrC2, that is an outlier in terms of IDs as compared to the rest of the runs, especially the rest of the runs from the group. However, based just off the total number of IDs, we can’t tell if we should remove this run or not from our analysis. Let’s look at some other metrics, specifically: Charge state distribution Intensity distribution CV distribution at each “biological level” (precursor, peptide, protein group) 3.4.2.2 Charge State Distribution diann_results %>% select(Run, group, Precursor.Id, Precursor.Charge) %>% count(Run, group, Precursor.Charge) %>% group_by(Run, group) %>% mutate(total_precursors = sum(n)) %>% group_by(Run, group, Precursor.Charge) %>% reframe(charge_per = n/total_precursors * 100) %>% mutate(Precursor.Charge := fct_inorder(factor(Precursor.Charge)), Run := factor(Run, levels = unique(str_sort(Run, numeric = T)))) %>% ggplot(aes(x = Run, y = charge_per, fill = Precursor.Charge)) + geom_col(color = 'black', linewidth = 0.75) + facet_grid(cols = vars(group), scales = 'free') + labs(y = 'Percentage of Precursors', title = 'Percentage of Precursors at Specific Charge Among All IDs') + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 75, hjust = 1)) All runs follow a similar pattern of charge distribution, indicating ionization was consistent across the experiment Intensity Distribution Un-normalized Intensity diann_results %>% select(Run, group, Precursor.Id, Precursor.Quantity) %>% filter(Precursor.Quantity > 0) %>% mutate(Precursor.Quantity = log2(Precursor.Quantity)) %>% ggplot(aes(x = Precursor.Quantity, color = Run)) + geom_freqpoly(linewidth = 0.75, bins = 25) + facet_grid(cols = vars(group), scales = 'free') + labs(x = 'Un-normalized Precursor Abundance (log2)', title = 'Distribution of Un-normalized Precursor Abundances') It would appear that the msrC2 sample might have had lower coverage due to a decrease in the total amount of peptide loaded onto the HPLC column, which would in turn lead to a decrease in overall abundances. Luckily, normalization can sometimes make up for differences like these. Let’s look to see if DIA-NN normalization helped. 3.4.2.2.1 Normalized Intensity diann_results %>% select(Run, group, Precursor.Id, Precursor.Normalised) %>% filter(Precursor.Normalised > 0) %>% mutate(Precursor.Normalised = log2(Precursor.Normalised)) %>% ggplot(aes(x = Precursor.Normalised, color = Run)) + geom_freqpoly(linewidth = 0.75, bins = 25) + facet_grid(cols = vars(group), scales = 'free') + labs(x = 'Normalized Precursor Abundance (log2)', title = 'Distribution of Normalized Precursor Abundances') While the left edge of the curve is narrower, msrC2 now mostly agrees with the rest of the group. This would imply that by using the normalized precursor quantities, we shouldn’t see too much effect from the decreased material loaded in msrC2 on the downstream analyses. Just to be sure, let’s check the protein abudances, which is calculated using the normalized precursor abundances. diann_results %>% distinct(Run, group, Protein.Group, PG.MaxLFQ) %>% filter(PG.MaxLFQ > 0) %>% mutate(PG.MaxLFQ = log2(PG.MaxLFQ)) %>% ggplot(aes(x = PG.MaxLFQ, color = Run)) + geom_freqpoly(linewidth = 0.75, bins = 25) + facet_grid(cols = vars(group), scales = 'free') + labs(x = 'Protein Abundance (log2)', title = 'Distribution of Protein Abundances') Once “wrapping up” to the protein level, any differences in abundances is now even smaller. Therefore, we can be a little more confident that we can use all our data in downstream analyses. But just to be sure, we’re going to look at one more metric. CV Distribution We are also going to measure the coefficient of variation (CV, standard deviation divided by the mean) across each “biological level” to see if there are any groups what have inconsistent quantification across all reps of that group. We will also be measuring the CV across all 15 samples, regardless of biological group. This is a good sanity check to always perform; if your experiment was properly designed, the CV between replicates within the same group should be lower than the CV between all the samples. Precursor Level diann_results %>% distinct(Precursor.Id, Run, group, Precursor.Normalised) %>% filter(Precursor.Normalised > 0) %>% group_by(Precursor.Id) %>% mutate(cv_combined = sd(Precursor.Normalised, na.rm = T)/mean(Precursor.Normalised, na.rm = T) * 100) %>% ungroup() %>% group_by(group, Precursor.Id) %>% mutate(cv = sd(Precursor.Normalised, na.rm = T)/mean(Precursor.Normalised, na.rm = T) * 100) %>% ungroup() %>% distinct(group, Precursor.Normalised, cv_combined, cv) %>% drop_na() %>% pivot_longer(cols = starts_with('cv'), names_to = 'type', values_to = 'cv') %>% mutate(type = ifelse(type == 'cv', group, 'All Groups Combined')) %>% mutate(type = fct_relevel(as.factor(type), 'All Groups Combined')) %>% ggplot(aes(x = type, y = cv)) + geom_violin(aes(fill = type), na.rm = T) + geom_boxplot(na.rm = T, width = 0.1) + labs(title = 'Distribution of Coefficient of Variation: Precursor-level', x = '', y = 'Coefficient of Variation (%)', fill = 'Group') + scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 200)) For all groups, the CV of the normalized precursors are all below the intra-group CV, including the msrC group, which had the one outlier. However, based on our earlier graphs, we should expect the CV of that group to decrease as we “go up” in biological level to the protein level. Protein Level diann_results %>% distinct(Protein.Group, Run, group, PG.MaxLFQ) %>% filter(PG.MaxLFQ > 0) %>% group_by(Protein.Group) %>% mutate(cv_combined = sd(PG.MaxLFQ, na.rm = T)/mean(PG.MaxLFQ, na.rm = T) * 100) %>% ungroup() %>% group_by(group, Protein.Group) %>% mutate(cv = sd(PG.MaxLFQ, na.rm = T)/mean(PG.MaxLFQ, na.rm = T) * 100) %>% ungroup() %>% distinct(group, PG.MaxLFQ, cv_combined, cv) %>% drop_na() %>% pivot_longer(cols = starts_with('cv'), names_to = 'type', values_to = 'cv') %>% mutate(type = ifelse(type == 'cv', group, 'All Groups Combined')) %>% mutate(type = fct_relevel(as.factor(type), 'All Groups Combined')) %>% ggplot(aes(x = type, y = cv)) + geom_violin(aes(fill = type), na.rm = T) + geom_boxplot(na.rm = T, width = 0.1) + labs(title = 'Distribution of Coefficient of Variation: Protein-level', x = '', y = 'Coefficient of Variation (%)', fill = 'Group') + scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 200)) While the CV of the msrC group did go down, so did the intra-group CV. So much so that it is now lower than the msrC CV. This means that the msrC group has too much variability to reliably compare to other groups. However, we know that one run in particular is causing this variability. So what would have if we were to remove that run (msrC2)? diann_results %>% distinct(Protein.Group, Run, group, PG.MaxLFQ) %>% filter(Run != 'msrC2') %>% filter(PG.MaxLFQ > 0) %>% group_by(Protein.Group) %>% mutate(cv_combined = sd(PG.MaxLFQ, na.rm = T)/mean(PG.MaxLFQ, na.rm = T) * 100) %>% ungroup() %>% group_by(group, Protein.Group) %>% mutate(cv = sd(PG.MaxLFQ, na.rm = T)/mean(PG.MaxLFQ, na.rm = T) * 100) %>% ungroup() %>% distinct(group, PG.MaxLFQ, cv_combined, cv) %>% drop_na() %>% pivot_longer(cols = starts_with('cv'), names_to = 'type', values_to = 'cv') %>% mutate(type = ifelse(type == 'cv', group, 'All Groups Combined')) %>% mutate(type = fct_relevel(as.factor(type), 'All Groups Combined')) %>% ggplot(aes(x = type, y = cv)) + geom_violin(aes(fill = type), na.rm = T) + geom_boxplot(na.rm = T, width = 0.1) + labs(title = 'Distribution of Coefficient of Variation: Protein-level', x = '', y = 'Coefficient of Variation (%)', fill = 'Group') + scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 200)) Our msrC group now has really low CVs, similar to the other runs. Therefore in downstream analyses, we have to remember to remove that run before doing anything else.

3.5 Filtering DIA-NN Results Now that we have a good grasp on how our data looks, we’re going to remove any low-quality and contaminant precursors from our dataset before we move forward with any analyses. Additionally, since we know that msrC2 is an outlier, we are going to move any IDs from that sample as well. We will be removing “low quality” IDs through use of the various Q.Value columns and quantification Quality columns. We will be removing contaminant IDs through using the “cRAPome” database that we included in our spectral library Recall from the previous chapter that using the cRAPome will add common mass spec contaminants to our spectral library. Anything that matches to one of those proteins will then have “cRAP” in any of: Protein.Ids, Protein.Group, Protein.Ids, and/or Genes diann_filter <- diann_results %>% filter(Run != 'msrC2') %>% #remove the outlier run #remove the poor quality IDs filter(Q.Value <= 0.01 & Lib.Q.Value <= 0.01 & PG.Q.Value <= 0.05 & Lib.PG.Q.Value <= 0.05 & Quantity.Quality > 0 & PG.MaxLFQ.Quality > 0) %>% #remove the contaminant IDs filter(!grepl('cRAP', Protein.Ids) & !grepl('cRAP', Protein.Group) & !grepl('cRAP', Protein.Ids) & !grepl('cRAP', Genes)) %>% select(c(Run, group, Modified.Sequence, Protein.Group, Genes, PG.MaxLFQ)) To give an idea as to the number of IDs we removed: full_join(x = diann_results %>% group_by(Run) %>% mutate(n_precursors_preFilter = n()) %>% distinct(Run, n_precursors_preFilter), y = diann_results %>% distinct(Run, Protein.Group) %>% group_by(Run) %>% mutate(n_proteins_preFilter = n()) %>% distinct(Run, n_proteins_preFilter), by = 'Run') %>% full_join(y = diann_filter %>% group_by(Run) %>% mutate(n_precursors_postFilter = n()) %>% distinct(Run, n_precursors_postFilter), by = 'Run') %>% full_join(y = diann_filter %>% distinct(Run, Protein.Group) %>% group_by(Run) %>% mutate(n_proteins_postFilter = n()) %>% distinct(Run, n_proteins_postFilter), by = 'Run') %>% arrange(Run) ## # A tibble: 15 × 5 ## # Groups: Run [15] ## Run n_precursors_preFilter n_proteins_preFilter n_precursors_postFilter ## <chr> <int> <int> <int> ## 1 WT1 51157 3503 43807 ## 2 WT2 51907 3520 44933 ## 3 WT3 52198 3559 44872 ## 4 bisC1 50325 3514 43028 ## 5 bisC2 50622 3520 43670 ## 6 bisC3 53684 3574 46281 ## 7 msrA1 52689 3576 45219 ## 8 msrA2 51116 3515 43695 ## 9 msrA3 52612 3539 45249 ## 10 msrB1 52338 3547 45505 ## 11 msrB2 50947 3535 43301 ## 12 msrB3 51858 3520 44671 ## 13 msrC1 53000 3593 45673 ## 14 msrC2 38110 3106 NA ## 15 msrC3 53577 3603 46314 ## # ℹ 1 more variable: n_proteins_postFilter <int>
3.6 Prepare Data for Differential Expression Analysis (t-test)

3.6 Prepare Data for Differential Expression Analysis (t-test) There are just a few more things we need to do before our data is ready for statistical analysis. More specifically, we need to: Filter data based off total number of peptides and completeness Replace missing values through imputation 3.6.1 Filter Based off Number of Peptides and Missing Values When dealing with biological data, especially when dealing with gene knockouts, it would not be unexpected for some proteins to not be detected at all in some groups compared to others. This could be a result of a combination of two things: The protein is either down-regulated or not even expressed in one group The level of the protein is beneath the limit of detection of our analytical method (in this case, the mass spectrometer) The main issue that arises from this is that some proteins will be detected in some samples/groups and not others. The samples the protein was not detected in will have a value like 0 of NA denoting that there is no value present for that protein in that sample. This is called a missing value. R does not handle NA very well (or any programming language for that matter), so when we try to perform any kind of statistical analysis on groups where NA is present, it either will not work or will look “funky”. There are several ways to circumvent this program, including: Removing any proteins with a missing value Replacing missing values with a constant number Imputing the missing value using some mathematical formula The last method, imputation is usually how we go ahead with handling missing values. Specifically, a method where a random, low-value number is assigned to a missing value (at the protein level). More specifically: The protein abundances are converted to log2 (to make the data normally distributed) A standard curve is made from the log2 protein abundances That standard curve is shifted by a standard deviation we determine (we default to 1.8 standard deviations) The new, shifted curve is “shrunk” to a width of the original curve that we determine (we default to 0.3 the width of the original curve) A random number from this new, shifted and shrunken curve is assigned to each missing value A key assumption to this method is that any missing values correspond to low-abundance proteins that fall below the limit of detection of our mass spectrometer. However, before we impute, we perform one more quality check to make sure what we’re imputing isn’t artificially creating biological phenomena. Specifically, we set thresholds for each protein so that each protein: Has multiple peptides that map to that protein (no “one hit wonders”) There are multiple replicates within at least one group with multiple peptides diann_peptide_counts <- diann_filter %>% group_by(Run, Protein.Group, Genes) %>% summarise(n_peptides = n(), .groups = 'drop') %>% left_join(y = diann_filter %>% distinct(Run, group, Protein.Group, Genes, PG.MaxLFQ), by = c('Run', 'Protein.Group', 'Genes')) diann_peptide_filter_list <- diann_peptide_counts %>% drop_na() %>% select(-PG.MaxLFQ) %>% filter(n_peptides >= 2) %>% group_by(group, Protein.Group, Genes) %>% summarise(n_reps = n(), .groups = 'drop') %>% filter(n_reps >= 2) %>% select(-n_reps) diann_peptide_filtered_data <- inner_join(x = diann_peptide_counts, y = diann_peptide_filter_list, by = c('group', 'Protein.Group', 'Genes')) diann_peptide_filtered_data ## # A tibble: 41,702 × 6 ## Run Protein.Group Genes n_peptides group PG.MaxLFQ ## <chr> <chr> <chr> <int> <chr> <dbl> ## 1 WT1 O52982 yfjS 2 WT 86634. ## 2 WT1 P00350 gnd 43 WT 963161664 ## 3 WT1 P00363 frdA 63 WT 650691136 ## 4 WT1 P00370 gdhA 30 WT 40558036 ## 5 WT1 P00393 ndh 38 WT 117415024 ## 6 WT1 P00448 sodA 16 WT 464933792 ## 7 WT1 P00452 nrdA 48 WT 132433664 ## 8 WT1 P00490 malP 62 WT 201849968 ## 9 WT1 P00509 aspC 37 WT 591304576 ## 10 WT1 P00547 thrB 8 WT 36680244 ## # ℹ 41,692 more rows We now have a data frame with a new column containing the number of peptides that were identified for each protein in each sample. And was filtered so that every protein was quantified using 2 or more peptides. AND has at least one group with at least two replicates with that protein present. So now we are a little more confident in our dataset that the proteins that are still present were at least quantified in at least one of our groups. The last thing we need to do before imputing missing values is to make our data “wide”, which just means that instead of each run being on its own row, each row is now a single protein and each column is the protein abundance in that sample. At this point, we will now see NA if there is a missing value. diann_peptide_filtered_wide <- diann_peptide_filtered_data %>% select(-c(n_peptides, group)) %>% mutate(PG.MaxLFQ = log2(PG.MaxLFQ)) %>% pivot_wider(names_from = 'Run', values_from = 'PG.MaxLFQ') diann_peptide_filtered_wide ## # A tibble: 3,039 × 16 ## Protein.Group Genes WT1 WT2 WT3 bisC1 bisC2 bisC3 msrA1 msrA2 msrA3 ## <chr> <chr> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> ## 1 O52982 yfjS 16.4 16.6 16.2 NA NA NA NA NA NA ## 2 P00350 gnd 29.8 29.7 29.7 29.8 29.7 29.7 29.5 29.8 29.6 ## 3 P00363 frdA 29.3 29.2 29.2 29.2 29.1 29.1 29.1 29.3 29.2 ## 4 P00370 gdhA 25.3 25.3 25.3 25.3 25.4 25.3 25.1 25.1 25.2 ## 5 P00393 ndh 26.8 26.9 26.8 26.9 26.7 26.8 27.0 26.9 26.8 ## 6 P00448 sodA 28.8 29.7 29.7 30.1 29.7 29.5 28.8 28.3 29.4 ## 7 P00452 nrdA 27.0 27.0 26.9 27.1 27.0 27.0 26.7 26.8 26.9 ## 8 P00490 malP 27.6 27.6 27.6 27.8 27.7 27.8 27.5 27.6 27.6 ## 9 P00509 aspC 29.1 29.1 29.1 29.1 29.0 29.0 29.0 29.1 29.1 ## 10 P00547 thrB 25.1 25.1 25.2 24.6 24.3 24.3 24.9 25.4 24.8 ## # ℹ 3,029 more rows ## # ℹ 5 more variables: msrB1 <dbl>, msrB2 <dbl>, msrB3 <dbl>, msrC1 <dbl>, ## # msrC3 <dbl> 3.6.2 Impute Missing Values With this wide data frame, we will now impute missing values. Chapter 4 Performing Differential Expression Statistics (t-test) In this chapter, you’ll learn: How to perform a moderated t-test on proteomic data to obtain: log2 fold changes in protein expression levels p-values and adjusted p-values for changes in protein expression levels Pre-requisites: A basic understanding of R and RStudio A set of of filtered proteomic data with no missing values The following R packages installed from CRAN (recall, install.packages("<package_name>"): tidyverse BiocManager rlang The limma R package installed from Bioconductor (recall, BiocManager::install("limma"))

4.1 Background In most global proteomic experiments, the main question being asked is: “what protein, or set of proteins, expression level(s) are changing in response to some kind of stimulus (e.g., drug treatment, gene knockout, environmental stressor, etc.)?” To answer this question, we usually will collect samples (e.g., cultured cells, tissue, plasma, etc.) from one group representing the background proteome (the control) and from another group that has been exposed to our stimulus of interest, measure the protein levels from both groups and compare those levels. However, biology is usually (actually, almost always) very complex and sources of variation can present themselves at every stage of the experiment. For this reason, most experiments will employ replication to try and “reduce” the variation. In other words, if we do not observe differences in protein expression levels between different replicates of the same group, but those same proteins have differences in expression between the different groups, we can be more confident the observed expression change is reflective of a true biological phenomenon. Luckily for us, statisticians have developed several methods to standardize the comparison between various groups, while controlling for inter-group variation; the one we’ll be focusing on in this tutorial is called the t-test, specifically what’s called a moderated t-test, as employed in the R package limma (https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/limma.html) Types of t-tests Within the world of statistics, there a multiple flavors of t-tests, the “appropriate” one to use depending upon your data and experimental design. Those types are: Unpaired T-Test: Also know as an independent samples t-test. Used when two completely separate and distinct groups are being compared (e.g., 3 cell culture plates treated with DMSO and 3 different plates treated with a drug). Assesses whether the difference between the averages of these two groups is larger than you would expect by random chance. Paired T-Test: Used when the observations in one group are directly linked to the observations in the other group (e.g., measuring a patient’s blood pressure before and after medication) A paired t-test is more “powerful” than an unpaired t-test and is useful when noise can vary dramatically between individuals (samples, replicates, subjects, etc.) as each individual acts as their own control. Moderated T-Test: When performing thousands of t-tests simultaneously (like one would do in an omics experiment), some genes will accidentally display unusual variances simply by chance. Through use of an empirical Bayes method, this kind of t-test shrinks individual gene variances towards a pooled, global variance estimate. A moderated t-test prevents false positives and increases statistical power for experiments with low sample sizes. Refer to the table below for a table detailing the differences between these three t-tests: Feature Unpaired T-Test Paired T-Test Moderated T-Test Data Structure Individual groups (e.g., control vs. treated) Dependent pairs (e.g., same person before/after) Thousands of simultaneous variables (e.g., proteins) Question Is the difference between the two group means significant? Is the mean of the differences significantly different than 0? Is the effect size (e.g., fold-change in expression) for each variable significant? Variance Handling Analyzes variance between the two independent groups Analyzes the variances within each pair Uses emperical Bayes to “squeeze” or moderate extreme variance estimates Optimal Use Case A/B testing, clinical trials Repeated measures and matched-pair experimental designs Global proteomics, Bulk RNA-seq, gene expression microarrays Now that we have a little understanding of what we’re trying to accomplish, let’s move ahead with preparing our data for a moderated t-test.

4.2 Prepare the t-test The first thing we need to do is design all the parameters for our t-test. In other words, declare what groups we want compared to each other. 4.2.1 Create the Comparison Table #Create a list of all conditions all_conditions <- imputed_data_df %>% distinct(group) %>% pull() all_conditions ## [1] "WT" "bisC" "msrA" "msrB" "msrC" We then need to create a data frame containing all possible condition comparisons all_combinations <- as_tibble(t(combn(all_conditions, m = 2)), .name_repair = ~ make.names(., unique = T)) %>% rename(V1 = X, V2 = X.1) %>% mutate(combinations = paste0(V1, '_vs_', V2)) %>% pivot_longer(cols = c(V1, V2), names_to = 'name', values_to = 'group') %>% select(-name) %>% group_by(group) %>% mutate(comparison = list(combinations)) %>% distinct(comparison, group) all_combinations ## # A tibble: 5 × 2 ## # Groups: group [5] ## comparison group ## <list> <chr> ## 1 <chr [4]> WT ## 2 <chr [4]> bisC ## 3 <chr [4]> msrA ## 4 <chr [4]> msrB ## 5 <chr [4]> msrC all_combinations$comparison ## [[1]] ## [1] "WT_vs_bisC" "WT_vs_msrA" "WT_vs_msrB" "WT_vs_msrC" ## ## [[2]] ## [1] "WT_vs_bisC" "bisC_vs_msrA" "bisC_vs_msrB" "bisC_vs_msrC" ## ## [[3]] ## [1] "WT_vs_msrA" "bisC_vs_msrA" "msrA_vs_msrB" "msrA_vs_msrC" ## ## [[4]] ## [1] "WT_vs_msrB" "bisC_vs_msrB" "msrA_vs_msrB" "msrB_vs_msrC" ## ## [[5]] ## [1] "WT_vs_msrC" "bisC_vs_msrC" "msrA_vs_msrC" "msrB_vs_msrC" This data frame is made up of two columns: group: A string denoting the condition, or group (in this case, the E. coli genotype) comparison: a list made up of all the possible group comparison for the specific group, with each group separated by “vs”. For example, the group WT contains the following comparisons: “WT_vs_bisC” “WT_vs_msrA” “WT_vs_msrB” “WT_vs_msrC” While the group bisC contains: “WT_vs_bisC” “bisC_vs_msrA” “bisC_vs_msrB” “bisC_vs_msrC” And so on for the other 3 genotypes 4.2.2 Prepare Our Data Using the Comparison Table We are nor almost ready to perform a t-test. Recall that we will be using our data frame containing the imputed values. Before going any further, we need to add a new column to that data frame: a comlumn containing replicate number imputed_data_df <- imputed_data_df %>% mutate(rep = as.integer(str_sub(Run, -1))) Now that our data frame is ready, we will move it around so it’s in a form that is full ready for limma. The main we need to do for this is to each each comparison we want to have evaluated. ttest_data_prep <- imputed_data_df %>% ungroup() %>% distinct(Protein.Group, group, rep, PG.MaxLFQ) %>% complete(nesting(group, rep), Protein.Group) %>% group_by(Protein.Group, group) %>% mutate(n_detect = sum(!is.na(PG.MaxLFQ))) %>% mutate(n_replicates = n()) %>% ungroup() %>% left_join(all_combinations, by = 'group') %>% unnest(comparison) %>% unite(group:rep, col = 'Run', sep = '_', remove = FALSE) ttest_data_prep ## # A tibble: 170,184 × 8 ## Run group rep Protein.Group PG.MaxLFQ n_detect n_replicates comparison ## <chr> <chr> <int> <chr> <dbl> <int> <int> <chr> ## 1 bisC_1 bisC 1 O52982 19.3 3 3 WT_vs_bisC ## 2 bisC_1 bisC 1 O52982 19.3 3 3 bisC_vs_msrA ## 3 bisC_1 bisC 1 O52982 19.3 3 3 bisC_vs_msrB ## 4 bisC_1 bisC 1 O52982 19.3 3 3 bisC_vs_msrC ## 5 bisC_1 bisC 1 P00350 29.8 3 3 WT_vs_bisC ## 6 bisC_1 bisC 1 P00350 29.8 3 3 bisC_vs_msrA ## 7 bisC_1 bisC 1 P00350 29.8 3 3 bisC_vs_msrB ## 8 bisC_1 bisC 1 P00350 29.8 3 3 bisC_vs_msrC ## 9 bisC_1 bisC 1 P00363 29.2 3 3 WT_vs_bisC ## 10 bisC_1 bisC 1 P00363 29.2 3 3 bisC_vs_msrA ## # ℹ 170,174 more rows Now we have a data frame with all our protein expression levels alnog with which group they belong to and what comparison we want to perform. 4.3 Create a T-Test Results Table Now that we have performed our moderated t-test, we need to actually extract the data out of it and store it as a format easier to work with (something like a data frame). We can do this through limma::topTable() ttest_data <- map_dfr(.x = names, .f = ~ topTable(moderated_t_test_fit3, coef = .x, number = Inf, confint = T, sort.by = 'p') %>% rownames_to_column('Protein.Group') %>% mutate(comparison = .x)) %>% mutate(comparison = str_replace_all(comparison, pattern = '^x|(?<=_vs_)x', replacement = '')) %>% dplyr::rename(log2fc = 'logFC', ci_l = 'CI.L', ci_r = 'CI.R', pval = 'P.Value', adj_pval = 'adj.P.Val', mean_abundance = 'AveExpr') %>% dplyr::select(-c(t, B)) This data frame is now ready for any kind of differential expression analysis you would like to perform downstream of this, including visualization by volcano plots, heatmaps, and gene ontology enrichment, which we’ll be doing in the next chapter.

Chapter 5 Visualizing Proteomic Data In this chapter, you’ll learn: Various ways to visualize proteomic data, including: Sample heatmap and hierarchical clustering Principle component analysis (PCA) Protein heatmap Volcano plot Gene Ontology (GO) analysis Pre-requisites: A basic understanding of R and RStudio A set of of proteomic data with group differences calculated through a t-test The following R packages installed from CRAN (recall, install.packages("<package_name>"): tidyverse BiocManager plotly dendextend ggforce ggrepel RColorBrewer gridExtra rio pheatmap rbioapi dendextend seriation reactable -reactablefmtr The following R packages installed from Bioconductor (recall, BiocManager::install("<package_name>")): ComplexHeatmap InteractiveComplexHeatmap ensembldb org.Sc.sgd.db (Note: This is the yeast Ensembl database. In order to get a different species database), you are going to have to download the library for that species as well.

We can then apply these themes to all of our plots by simply calling plot_theme instead of typing all these theming components for each plot. plot_theme <- theme_bw() + theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 20), axis.title.x = element_text(size = 15), axis.text.y = element_text(size = 15), axis.text.x = element_text(size = 12, angle = 75, hjust = 1), axis.title.y = element_text(size = 15), legend.title = element_text(size = 15), legend.text = element_text(size = 15))

5.2 Replicate Comparisons In order to properly ensure our experimental design was well thought out, we should evaluate how all of our samples compare to each other. We will be doing this several different ways: Clustering: Cluster all our samples according to the Spearman correlation of our overall protein abundances and draw a heatmap of those correlations. Ideally, all of the replicates from the same group will be more clustered with the other reps from the same group than with any samples from other groups Principal Component Analysis (PCA): A dimensionality reduction technique that can be used for extracting information from high-dimensional space (i.e., a biological experiment where variation can stem from a large number of variables) by projecting it into a lower-dimensional sub-space (i.e. a very few number of components that can readily be quantified and, ideally, subsequently identified). Dimensions are nothing but features that represent the data. Ideally, all of the replicates from the same group will be more clustered with the other reps from the same group than with any samples from other groups Indicates that the major sources of variation are coming from the biological groups than from any source of variation that was applied across all the samples (e.g., sample prep or data acquisition) 5.2.1 Sample Heatmap and Hierarchical Clustering 5.2.1.1 Prepare the Heatmap Annotation Set the Heatmap Annotation Scheme To properly connect the heatmap to our samples and groups, we need to get just a list of each sample and the group it belongs too (akin to the metadata). It also needs to samples as row names (Currently is sample name in each row with the column name ‘sample’). smpl_htmp_annotation <- sample_level %>% ungroup() %>% dplyr::rename(Condition = 'group') %>% distinct(sample, Condition) %>% tibble::column_to_rownames(var = 'sample') smpl_htmp_annotation ## We calculate a matrix that summarizes how all of our variables (i.e., the abundance of each protein in each sample) relate to one another. Break this matrix down into two separate aspects: direction and magnitude (Vectorman would be so proud). We can then understand the directions (the components) of our data and its magnitude (i.e., how “important” each direction is). 5.2.2.1 Prepare the PCA Data As the description above implies, we need to make a matrix of the magnitude of each direction (component). To do this, we first just need to make a matrix of our protein abundances, which we’ll be using our sample comparison data frame we made at the beginning of this chapter. pca_input <- sample_level %>% ungroup() %>% distinct(sample, protein_group, log2_abundance) %>% group_by(sample, protein_group) %>% summarise(intensity = sum(log2_abundance), .groups = 'drop') %>% #If we have multiple protein_groups per sample, we are simply going to add them together pivot_wider(names_from = sample, values_from = intensity) %>% drop_na() %>% dplyr::select(-protein_group) %>% t() #Make the data into a matrix pca_input[,1:6] ## 14 -1.1836086 1.587354e-13 msrC3 MsrC KO One other important piece of data prcomp() provides us is the standard deviation of the total experiment “explained” by each component. By adding up enough of our components, the variation of our experiment should eventually be completely explained. This information is stored in pca$sdev, which we will extract and again make into a data frame. pca_sdev_df <- as.data.frame(pca$sdev) pca_sdev_df ## pca$sdev ## 1 2.032677e+01 ## 2 1.246566e+01 ## 3 1.060359e+01 ## 4 8.806650e+00 ## 5 6.859006e+00 ## 6 4.814363e+00 ## 7 4.292032e+00 ## 8 4.147293e+00 ## 9 3.516503e+00 ## 10 3.345701e+00 ## 11 3.216724e+00 ## 12 3.045563e+00 ## 13 2.648766e+00 ## 14 1.653466e-13 We will then measure the percent variace each component captures from our dataset: 1- Retrieve the standard deviation of each component 2- Square the standard deviation to calculate the eigenvalues (the variance of each component, pca$sdev^2) 3- Calculate the total variance across all components combined (`sum(pca$sdev^2)) 4- Divide each component’s variance by the totalo variance to get a proportion (/) 5- Convert the proporation into a percentage (*100) pca_sdev_df <- pca_sdev_df %>% mutate(percent_variance = (pca$sdev^2/sum(pca$sdev^2)*100), dimension = row.names(.)) %>% mutate(dimension = factor(.data$dimension, levels = unique(str_sort(.data$dimension, numeric = TRUE)))) pca_sdev_df ## pca$sdev percent_variance dimension ## 1 2.032677e+01 4.517541e+01 1 ## 2 1.246566e+01 1.699009e+01 2 ## 3 1.060359e+01 1.229338e+01 3 ## 4 8.806650e+00 8.479823e+00 4 ## 5 6.859006e+00 5.143842e+00 5 ## 6 4.814363e+00 2.534212e+00 6 ## 7 4.292032e+00 2.014147e+00 7 ## 8 4.147293e+00 1.880593e+00 8 ## 9 3.516503e+00 1.352033e+00 9 ## 10 3.345701e+00 1.223882e+00 10 ## 11 3.216724e+00 1.131339e+00 11 ## 12 3.045563e+00 1.014146e+00 12 ## 13 2.648766e+00 7.671008e-01 13 ## 14 1.653466e-13 2.989205e-27 14 5.2.2.4 Plot the PCA Results Now that we have the components measures and total variance, we can make our PCA plots. We will be making three total plots. 1- A static PCA plot each with the first component (PC1) as the x-axis and the second component (PC2) as the y-axis, with each genotype having a different color 2- A dynamic PCA plot each with the first component (PC1) as the x-axis and the second component (PC2) as the y-axis, again with each genotype having a different color. Now when you hover over each point, a pop-up box will appear, showing the sample matching to that point. This can be used to see which specific samples might be considered an outlier of the rest of the group, if there are any. 3- A Scree plot: a simple line segment plot that show the eigenvalues for each individual principal component. The individual components are the x-axis eigenvalues are the y-axis. The main purpose of a scree plot is to determine the number of principal components to retain in downstream statistical analyses. Generally speaking, if you need more than 3 or 4 principal components to describe ~80% of your variance, a PCA might not be the best method to visualize your data, instead you might want to look into a more advanced dimensionality reduction technique (e.g., t-SNE or UMAP). Static PCA Plot ggplot(data = pca_df, mapping = aes(x = !!rlang::sym('PC1'), y = !!rlang::sym('PC2'), col = group, label = sample)) + geom_point(size = 3) + labs( title = "Principal Component Analysis", x = paste( 'PC1', "(", round( pca_sdev_df$percent_variance[pca_sdev_df$dimension==str_extract('PC1', "\\d")], 1), "%)" ), y = paste( 'PC2', "(", round( pca_sdev_df$percent_variance[pca_sdev_df$dimension==str_extract('PC2', "\\d")], 1), "%)" ), color = "Condition" ) + scale_color_manual(values = brewer.pal(n_distinct(pca_annotation$group), 'Set1')) + plot_theme Interactive PCA Plot ggplotly(ggplot(data = pca_df, mapping = aes(x = !!rlang::sym('PC1'), y = !!rlang::sym('PC2'), col = group, label = sample)) + geom_point(size = 3) + labs( title = "Principal Component Analysis", x = paste( 'PC1', "(", round( pca_sdev_df$percent_variance[pca_sdev_df$dimension==str_extract('PC1', "\\d")], 1), "%)" ), y = paste( 'PC2', "(", round( pca_sdev_df$percent_variance[pca_sdev_df$dimension==str_extract('PC2', "\\d")], 1), "%)" ), color = "Condition" ) + scale_color_manual(values = brewer.pal(n_distinct(pca_annotation$group), 'Set1')) + plot_theme) Scree Plot ggplot(data = pca_sdev_df %>% add_row(`pca$sdev` = as.double(0), percent_variance = as.double(0), dimension = as.factor(0), .before = 1) %>% mutate(cumulative_variance = cumsum(percent_variance), dimension = as.numeric(as.character(dimension))), mapping = aes(x = dimension, y = cumulative_variance, group = 1)) + geom_line() + geom_point() + geom_hline(yintercept = 80, color = 'red') + labs(x = "Number of Features", y = "Cumulative Variance Explained (%)") + plot_theme From all of the above plots, we can pretty confidently say our experiment is pretty sound and all the reps for each of our genotypes accurately represent each genotype. With this confidence, we can now move ahead and see if there are any proteins, or groups of proteins that are differentially expressed between the different genotypes.
5.3 Differential Expression WT vs MsrA KO: STRING Interacting Proteins

5.3 Differential Expression We are going to look how proteins are differentially expressed between genotypes through three main techniques: Protein expression heatmap- A heatmap displaying how abundant different proteins are between the genotypes Volcano plot- a dot plot showing the degree at which proteins are differentially expressed between groups and the confidence in that measurement (the results from the moderated t-test, p-value or adjusted p-value). 3- Gene Ontology (GO)- A method to determine if certain “groups” of proteins (gene ontologies) are together more highly expressed in one genotype when compared to another. 5.3.1 Protein Expression Heatmap For our protein expression heatmap, we will display only the top 100 most significant proteins, as determined by our moderated t-test (i.e., we will be selecting the proteins with the 100 lowest p-values). In reality, you can choose however many proteins you desire or use another criteria like log2 fold change. I personally like to use lowest p-values across the entire experiment. 5.3.1.1 Prepare The Data From our replicate comparison data frame, we will take the following columns: Protein group Sample Protein abundance (log2) protein_abundance_wide <- sample_level %>% distinct(protein_group, sample, log2_abundance) %>% pivot_wider(names_from = sample, values_from = log2_abundance) %>% drop_na() %>% column_to_rownames(var = 'protein_group') From our t-test data frame, we will sort the adjust p-value column (feel free to use something else if you desire) and sort it so the lowest p-values are first. Then we will select only the first 100 rows (slice_head(n=100)), and make a vector of the protein group IDs of those 100 proteins (pull(protein_group)). significant_proteins <- diff_expression_level %>% arrange(adj_pval) %>% slice_head(n = 100) %>% pull(protein_group) Make the protein abundance data frame, keep only the rows that match the gene names selected above ([significant_proteins, ]) and convert it to a matrix. heatmapData <- protein_abundance_wide[significant_proteins, ] 5.3.1.2 Clustering We will then cluster both the protein expression profiles and samples by the abundance of those 100 proteins. In our plot, we will only be using the sample clustering, but if we decide to, we can also add the protein clustering now. hclustGene <- hclust(dist(heatmapData), #Clusters the proteins by expression profile method = "complete") hclustSample <- hclust(dist(t(heatmapData)), #Clusters the samples method = "complete") 5.3.1.3 Prepare the Heatmap Annotation Like we did for our sample-level heatmap, we will need to make a series of annotation items to help us label of heatmap. Sample Colors The first thing we’ll do is create a tibble (prot_htmp_sample_columns) containing all the sample information. Specifically, the names of the samples (sample) and the genotype of that sample (group). Note: the text in the sample column has to match EXACTLY the text of the sample names in the matrix heatmapData while the group names can be whatever you want. prot_htmp_sample_columns <- tibble( sample = c('bisC1', 'bisC2', 'bisC3', 'msrA1', 'msrA2', 'msrA3', 'msrB1', 'msrB2', 'msrB3', 'msrC1', 'msrC3', 'WT1', 'WT2', 'WT3'), group = c(rep('bisC', 3), rep('msrA', 3), rep('msrB', 3), rep('msrC', 2), rep('WT', 3)) #Can be whatever you want to set it as ) prot_htmp_sample_columns ## # A tibble: 14 × 2 ## sample group ## <chr> <chr> ## 1 bisC1 bisC ## 2 bisC2 bisC ## 3 bisC3 bisC ## 4 msrA1 msrA ## 5 msrA2 msrA ## 6 msrA3 msrA ## 7 msrB1 msrB ## 8 msrB2 msrB ## 9 msrB3 msrB ## 10 msrC1 msrC ## 11 msrC3 msrC ## 12 WT1 WT ## 13 WT2 WT ## 14 WT3 WT We will then select a set of colors to label of samples, with the number of different colors equal to the number of groups (genotypes) we are analyzing. In this scenario, we are going to create a color to label each sample, and there will be a total of 5 unique colors. prot_htmp_sample_columns_colors <- hcl( h = seq(15, 375, length = 5 + 1), #The number of total groups we have in our experiment l = 65, c = 100)[1:5] #Select the number of colors equal to the number of groups we have in our experiment prot_htmp_sample_columns_colors ## [1] "#F8766D" "#A3A500" "#00BF7D" "#00B0F6" "#E76BF3" Heatmap Colors Next we need to define how we want the actual heatmap to be colored (i.e., the individual proteins). prot_map_colors <- brewer.pal(n = 11, name = 'RdBu') #The heatmap will use 11 total colors from the "RdBu" color palette to span the abundance profile. Feel free to play around with this number to better fit your data prot_map_colors <- colorRampPalette(prot_map_colors)(50) #We will span those 11 colors over a total of 50 ramps prot_map_colors = rev(prot_map_colors) Annotation Colors Next we will define how each group (genotype) will be colored. prot_htmp_groups_colors <- c(hcl(h = seq(15, 375, length = 5 + 1), l = 65, c = 100)[1:5]) #We take the same 5 colors used earlier for our group annotation and make them into a vector #We will formally describe our group annotations here prot_htmp_annotation_colors = list( Group = c('bisC' = prot_htmp_groups_colors[[1]], 'msrA' = prot_htmp_groups_colors[[2]], 'msrB' = prot_htmp_groups_colors[[3]], 'msrC' = prot_htmp_groups_colors[[4]], 'WT' = prot_htmp_groups_colors[[5]]) ) 5.3.1.4 Make the Heatmap Now that we have both our heatmap data, clustering data, and heatmap annotations, we can combine everything together using pheatmap() from ComplexHeatmap. Note: There are a ton of different possible settings. So feel free to mess around with the plot using the various arguments (hint, ?ComplexHeatmap::pheatmap()) pheatmap( as.matrix(heatmapData), color = prot_map_colors, fontsize_row = 5, main = 'Top 100 Differentially Expressed Protein (Lowest Adj. p-value)', annotation_col = prot_htmp_sample_columns, cutree_cols = 5, heatmap_legend_param = list(title = 'log2 Abundance', at = c(15, 20, 25, 30, 35)), annotation_colors = prot_htmp_annotation_colors ) 5.3.2 Volcano Plot Next we will work on how to display the results of the moderated t-test with a volcano plot. Its goal is to achieve a comprehensive and objective global network, including direct (physical) as well as indirect (functional) interactions. Protein expression levels were compared between bulk samples and each purified cell type. This was done for both stained and unstained populations.
